questions.question: Define one-to-one (injective) and onto (surjective) functions with examples from ℝ to ℝ.
keyPoints: One-to-one: f(a₁)=f(a₂) implies a₁=a₂, example f(x)=2x+1
Onto: for every y in codomain, ∃x such that f(x)=y, example f(x)=x³
Need to show counterexamples for non-injective/non-onto cases

questions.question: Let f: ℤ → ℤ be defined by f(n) = 2n + 1. Determine whether f is one-to-one and whether it is onto. Justify your answer.
keyPoints: Check one-to-one: assume f(m)=f(n) → 2m+1=2n+1 → m=n, so one-to-one
Check onto: solve 2n+1=y for any integer y → n=(y-1)/2, which is integer only when y is odd
Not onto ℤ because even integers have no pre-image
Conclusion: injective but not surjective

questions.question: State and explain the Pigeonhole Principle. How does it relate to one-to-one and onto functions?
keyPoints: If |A| > |B|, no function A→B can be one-to-one (pigeonhole principle)
If |A| < |B|, no function A→B can be onto
If |A| = |B|, a function is one-to-one iff it is onto
This is fundamental result about function properties

questions.question: What is a bijective function? Given an example of a bijective function from ℝ to ℝ and prove it is bijective.
keyPoints: Bijective = both one-to-one and onto
Example: f(x) = x³ or f(x) = 2x+3
For one-to-one: show f(x₁)=f(x₂) → x₁=x₂
For onto: given y, show x = ∛y satisfies f(x)=y

questions.question: Define the composition of two functions. If f: A→B and g: B→C are both one-to-one, prove that g∘f is one-to-one.
keyPoints: Composition: (g∘f)(a) = g(f(a))
Proof: assume (g∘f)(x₁) = (g∘f)(x₂)
Then g(f(x₁)) = g(f(x₂))
Since g is one-to-one: f(x₁)=f(x₂)
Since f is one-to-one: x₁=x₂
Therefore g∘f is one-to-one

questions.question: When does a function have an inverse? Find the inverse of f(x) = (x-1)/(x+1), x ≠ -1.
keyPoints: Inverse exists iff function is bijective
Solve for x: y(x+1) = x-1 → yx + y = x - 1

questions.question: What is the difference between domain, codomain, and range of a function? Illustrate with an example.
keyPoints: Domain: set of all possible inputs
Codomain: set specified as target (may be larger than range)
Range (Image): set of actual outputs achieved
Example: f: ℝ→ℤ where f(x)=⌊x⌋, domain=ℝ, codomain=ℤ, range=ℤ

questions.question: Let A = {1,2,3,4} and B = {a,b,c}. Consider the following functions from A to B and determine which are one-to-one and which are onto: (i) f(1)=a, f(2)=a, f(3)=b, f(4)=c (ii) g(1)=a, g(2)=b, g(3)=c, g(4)=a (iii) h(1)=a, h(2)=b, h(3)=c, h(4)=c
keyPoints: (i) f: not one-to-one (1,2→a), but onto (a,b,c all appear)
(ii) g: one-to-one (all distinct), onto (all elements appear) - BIJECTIVE
(iii) h: one-to-one (all distinct), not onto (c appears twice, b only once, a not at all) - need to check: actually onto? a,b appear - yes, c appears, so onto
For (iii): Check again - h(1)=a, h(2)=b, h(3)=c, h(4)=c. All a,b,c appear, so onto. One-to-one: all different - yes.

questions.question: Prove that if f: A→B and g: B→C are both onto functions, then g∘f: A→C is also onto. Is the converse true? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: Proof: Let c ∈ C. Since g is onto, ∃b ∈ B such that g(b)=c
Since f is onto, ∃a ∈ A such that f(a)=b
Then (g∘f)(a) = g(f(a)) = g(b) = c
Thus every c ∈ C has pre-image, so g∘f is onto
Converse is FALSE: g∘f onto does NOT imply f is onto
Counterexample: A={1}, B={a,b}, C={c}, f(1)=a, g(a)=g(b)=c. g∘f is onto but f is not onto (b has no pre-image)

questions.question: Determine whether each of the following functions from ℝ to ℝ is one-to-one, onto, or bijective: (a) f(x)=x² (b) f(x)=x³ (c) f(x)=eˣ (d) f(x)=sin x
keyPoints: (a) x²: NOT one-to-one (f(2)=f(-2)), NOT onto (negative outputs)
(b) x³: ONE-TO-ONE (strictly increasing, cubic is injective), ONTO ℝ (odd degree covers all reals), BIJECTIVE
(c) eˣ: ONE-TO-ONE (strictly increasing), NOT onto ℝ (only positive outputs), NOT bijective
(d) sin x: NOT one-to-one (sin(0)=sin(π)=0), NOT onto ℝ (only [-1,1]), NOT bijective

questions.question: Let f: ℝ → ℝ be defined by f(x) = 3x + 7. (i) Prove f is bijective (ii) Find the inverse function f⁻¹ (iii) Verify that f⁻¹(f(x)) = x
keyPoints: (i) One-to-one: f(x₁)=f(x₂) → 3x₁+7=3x₂+7 → 3x₁=3x₂ → x₁=x₂. Onto: let y∈ℝ, x=(y-7)/3 gives f(x)=y. Hence bijective
